build for permanency, saying con.


crete was the only material with


which this could be done. He sug-
gested that if the city council will
refer the matter to referendum
vote of the citizens it will learn


that 80 per cent favor concrete.
G. B. Dennis said he was for
seel, but would stand for concrete
if the concrete advocates will guar
antee such bridges do not cost over
% per cent more than steel.
City Commissioner OBrien, who
favors concrete, said Engineer Rails
ton had made an off hand estimate
of $706,000 on the Monroe street
bridge, but that he believed Mr.
Ralston would be surprised at how
much less the cost will be when he
makes his estimate IN detail.


w. S. Norman urged temporiz
ig policy, holding down to Ne orig.
inal 100000 estimate. He favored
stopping work on the Mission ave-
nue. bridge. His argument aroused
protest from the audience. Mr..
Norman urged the water isue for
prior consideration.


d. T. Ham viewed the question
from the standpoint of financial
conditions. The present bonded
debt enlarged by the loans needed
to carry on water and sewer eX
tensions now facing the city, would
bring the total to point where
a city cannot issue bonds for ex-
pensive concrete bridges, he said,
and have sufficient funds left to
secure a permanent water supply
when it becomes necessary. He
was willing that the laboring man
should have every dollar for public
Improvements that can be raised
by bonding the city, but he would
advise close management until the
needs of the next few years had
been weighed carefully.


Mr.. Ham spoke OF the present
burdens of taxation to men of fair
means, when some one in the audi
ence cried out, Don't you think
we laboring men pay any taxes at
ALL" Mr.. Ham said he knew the
burden of heavy taxation was as
hard on the man with $500 home
as on J. J. Browne, who paid $685.
600 in taxes every year.


Judge George Turner arose to
deny an imputation on his cOnnec-
ton with the bridge question. He
said he represented no corporation
or private interest in his stand on
the bridge question, simply enter
ing into the matter in his capacity
as a citizen and a taxpayer. He
urged caution in proceeding with
concrete construction and ques-
toned the advisability of building
of concrete until it's stability in
high bridge construction is more
established.


R. F. Collins, Charles Liftchild
and Professor Blair also argued in
favor of concrete.
